findings.problem: The repaired plan still does not give the resolver enough normalized input to enforce reviewer-spawn authorization mechanically. It requires authorization to depend on whether a standalone/module-root caller supplied the minimal review bundle, but the state model and minimal input surface omit both an explicit bundle-sufficiency signal and several required bundle fields.
findings.evidence: The authorization task says the resolver must consume whether the call is a module-root standalone invocation and whether the minimal review bundle has been provided, then distinguish `authorized_and_should_run_now`, `authorized_but_pause_requested`, and `not_authorized` (docs/review-flow/transition-resolver-execution-plan.md:219-247). But the later state model only defines `subject`, `session`, `review_result`, `predecessor`, `caller`, `paths`, and `cache`, with no `minimal_bundle_present` or equivalent authorization payload (docs/review-flow/transition-resolver-execution-plan.md:476-515). The 'Recommended Minimal Resolver Inputs' section also omits `risk_tier`, `evidence_paths_or_diff_scope`, and `findings_contract` even though they are required bundle fields in the shared contracts (docs/review-flow/transition-resolver-execution-plan.md:607-627; openspec/schemas/modules/review-loop/contracts/review-loop-core-v1.json:6-13; openspec/schemas/modules/review-loop/contracts/review-loop-standalone-adapter-v1.json:13-21).
findings.recommendation: Extend the normalized resolver input contract so authorization can be checked mechanically. Either include the required review-bundle fields directly (`risk_tier`, `evidence_paths_or_diff_scope`, `findings_contract`, plus subject binding) or add an explicit normalized authorization object such as `module_root_invocation`, `minimal_bundle_present`, and `bundle_field_validation` derived from those fields.

findings.problem: The plan says the resolver must block shell/`exec` substitution when built-in reviewer spawning should be used, but the normalized state it defines still lacks the runtime invocation/capability inputs needed to evaluate that rule. As written, the denial path is declared but not enforceable.
findings.evidence: The invocation-mode section requires the resolver to consume whether built-in subagent use is required, whether shell fallback is allowed, whether `fork_context=false` is mandatory, and whether only the minimal bundle may be passed (docs/review-flow/transition-resolver-execution-plan.md:249-278). Exception routing also lists `tool availability` as an input (docs/review-flow/transition-resolver-execution-plan.md:373-402), and the mechanical denial cases include 'built-in subagent required but caller tries to replace it with shell/`exec`' (docs/review-flow/transition-resolver-execution-plan.md:555-572). However, the actual state model and minimal input list contain no `requested_invocation_mode`, `built_in_subagent_available`, or equivalent capability/attempt fields (docs/review-flow/transition-resolver-execution-plan.md:476-515,607-627).
findings.recommendation: Add normalized invocation-state inputs, such as `requested_invocation_mode`, `built_in_subagent_available`, `reviewer_required`, and `provided_context_scope`, so the resolver can compare the attempted invocation path against policy and mechanically return `blocked` when shell/`exec` is being used as an improper substitute.
